disease.name: Surgical Site Infection (Wound Infection)
disease.note: SSI is the most common postoperative complication, typically presenting 5-7 days post-surgery. Classified as superficial incisional, deep incisional, or organ/space. In Kenyan surgical wards where wound class contamination is higher, delayed presentation, and lack of running water for hygiene contribute to rates exceeding 15% in clean-contaminated cases.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: If sepsis/crepitus/wound dehiscence -> SEVERE (deep SSI or necrotizing); else -> NON-SEVERE (superficial SSI)

history_questions.question: When did the wound start looking red or swollen?
Is there any pus or fluid coming from the wound?
Has the wound pain increased?
Do you have a fever?
Has the wound opened or separated?
Do you feel generally unwell or have chills?
What type of surgery did you have?
Was it an emergency or planned surgery?
Do you have diabetes?
Do you smoke?
Are you on any steroids or chemotherapy?
Did you receive antibiotics during surgery?
How is your appetite and nutrition?
Is the wound warm to touch?
Did you have a drain? What color is the drainage?
Have you had any previous wound infections?
Is the wound on the abdomen, chest, or limb?
When did you first notice the wound was red, swollen, or painful?
Is the redness spreading or staying in one place?
Has the wound opened up or separated at the edges?
Is there any foul smell coming from the wound?
Do you have diabetes? If yes, what was your most recent blood sugar reading?
Do you smoke cigarettes or use any tobacco products?
Are you on any steroid medications (prednisone, dexamethasone) or chemotherapy?
Do you have HIV? If yes, what is your CD4 count and are you on antiretroviral therapy?
Have you had any previous wound infections after surgery in the past?
What type of surgery did you have, and was it an emergency or planned?
Did you receive antibiotics around the time of your surgery?
Have you had any other infections recently (chest, urine, skin)?
Are you taking any traditional herbal medicines or supplements?
Do you have a drain in place, and what color is the fluid coming from it?
Have you had any episodes of coughing, sneezing, or vomiting that put strain on the wound?
What is your typical diet like? Do you eat enough protein (meat, eggs, beans)?
Do you have any allergies to antibiotics (penicillin, sulfa, etc.)?
Have you been out of bed and walking since surgery?
When did you first notice the wound looking red or leaking fluid?
What color is the wound drainage? Is it thin and clear or thick and yellow/green?
Does the wound have an unpleasant odor?
Is the wound more painful today than yesterday?
Do you have fever or chills?
Have you been on antibiotics? Which ones and for how long?
Do you have diabetes? What is your most recent blood sugar?
Do you smoke cigarettes or use any tobacco products?
Are you overweight or obese? What is your approximate weight?
Have you had any previous wound infections after other surgeries?
Did you have any pre-operative infections (dental, UTI, skin) before this surgery?
Was the original surgery an emergency or elective procedure?
Was there any contamination during surgery (bowel spillage, pus, dirty case)?
Do you have any prosthetic material (mesh, artificial joint, heart valve) that could be seeding?

examination_findings.finding: Erythema extending >2 cm from wound edge
Purulent discharge from wound
Wound induration (hardness)
Wound tenderness on palpation
Fever (temperature >38°C)
Wound dehiscence (gaping open)
Crepitus on palpation
Necrotic tissue or slough in wound
Serosanguinous or purulent drain output
Systemic signs: tachycardia, tachypnea
Lymphangitic streaking proximal to wound
Regional lymphadenopathy
Foul odor from wound
Surrounding skin blistering or bullae
Erythema extending >5 cm from wound edge (suggests deep SSI or spreading cellulitis)
Wound dehiscence with intact fascia (skin and subcutaneous separation only)
Complete wound dehiscence with fascial rupture and exposed viscera (evisceration -- surgical emergency)
Purulent discharge that is thick, yellowish-green, and foul-smelling
Serosanguinous discharge (may be early SSI or normal healing -- culture to differentiate)
Crepitus on palpation (gas in tissues -- emergency: gas-forming organism or necrotizing fasciitis)
Skin blistering, bullae, or ecchymosis surrounding wound (suggests necrotizing infection)
Wound edge necrosis (black, non-viable tissue requiring debridement)
Lymphangitic streaking (red lines tracking proximally from wound)
Regional lymphadenopathy (tender, enlarged lymph nodes in groin, axilla)
Fever >38.5C with wound signs (suggests systemic involvement)
Hypothermia (<36C) in a patient with wound infection (ominous sign -- overwhelming sepsis)
Leukocytosis (>15,000/mcL) or leukopenia (<4,000/mcL) with left shift
CRP >200 mg/L and rising (active infection marker)
Procalcitonin >2 ng/mL (bacterial etiology confirmed)
Hyperglycemia (glucose >180 mg/dL) in known diabetic or stress hyperglycemia in non-diabetic
Hypoalbuminemia (<25 g/L) suggests malnutrition contributing to poor wound healing
Obesity (BMI >30) visible on exam -- mechanical tension on wound closure
Wound probe passes below the fascia (deep SSI requiring operative exploration)

examFeatures.note: Suggests deep infection or technical failure

bedside_scores.name: CDC/NHSN SSI Risk Index
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score 0: low risk. Score 3: high risk (up to 13% SSI rate).

riskFactors.note: Common in emergency laparotomies for peritonitis in Kenya

subtypes.name: Superficial Incisional SSI
subtypes.management: Open wound (remove skin sutures/clips). Evacuate pus, break loculi. Wound swab for culture. Pack with saline-soaked gauze. Change dressing daily. Oral antibiotics if cellulitis: Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS or Clindamycin 300 mg QDS if penicillin-allergic. Secondary intention healing. Delayed primary closure at day 4-5 if wound clean.

subtypes.name: Deep Incisional SSI
subtypes.management: Urgent wound exploration in OR under general anesthesia. Open wound fully to assess fascia. Debride all necrotic tissue. If fascia intact: pack wound, NPWT if available, IV antibiotics. If fascia necrotic or dehisced: debride fascia, repair if possible, consider biologic mesh. Do NOT close skin. IV Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g QDS (or Meropenem 1 g TDS if ESBL suspected). Plan relook in 24-48 hours.

subtypes.name: Organ/Space SSI
subtypes.management: CT imaging to identify collection. CT-guided percutaneous drainage if accessible and safe. If not amenable to percutaneous drainage or patient unstable: re-laparotomy for source control. IV antibiotics tailored to culture. Start empiric: Meropenem 1 g TDS + Metronidazole 500 mg TDS. Sepsis resuscitation if indicated. ICU admission for septic shock.

discriminators: small (<1 cm) pustule at a suture entry point
erythema extending beyond wound in geometric pattern (shape of dressing)
ruleOutTests: Resolution with topical steroids and dressing change

discriminators: rapidly expanding painful ulcer with violaceous undermined edges
may have history of IBD or rheumatoid arthritis

bedside.name: Wound swab for culture and sensitivity

management.protocolReference: CDC/NHSN SSI Criteria; WSES Guidelines for Surgical Site Infections

resuscitation: If sepsis: IV fluids 30 mL/kg crystalloid
Assess need for wound exploration in OR
analgesia: IV Morphine if wound exploration needed
antibiotics: Superficial: Flucloxacillin 500 mg PO QDS or Clindamycin 300 mg PO QDS if penicillin-allergic

deep.step5: If fascia dehisced: repair fascia, consider mesh if clean

pathways.criteria: localized erythema, purulent discharge, no systemic features
pathways.treatment: Open wound, drain pus, pack daily. Wound swab. Oral antibiotics (Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS or Clindamycin if allergic). Review in 48 hours.

pathways.criteria: fascial involvement, wound dehiscence, fever, elevated WBC/CRP
pathways.treatment: Urgent wound exploration in OR. Debridement. IV antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g QDS). NPWT consideration. ICU if septic.

pathways.criteria: intra-abdominal collection on CT, persistent fever, ileus
pathways.treatment: CT-guided percutaneous drainage or re-laparotomy. IV antibiotics tailored to culture. Source control essential.

operative_templates.procedure: Wound Exploration and Debridement for Deep SSI
operative_templates.indication: Deep incisional SSI with suspected fascial involvement
steps: Remove all skin sutures or staples
If peritonitis: extend incision for full exploration
If fascia intact: consider delayed primary closure

watchFor: persistent small opening draining serous fluid

dischargeCriteria: Patient or carer trained to perform wound care at home
Follow-up appointment confirmed for wound review
Culture results reviewed and antibiotics rationalized

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Your surgical wound has become infected. Bacteria have entered the wound and caused inflammation and pus. We have opened the wound to drain the infection and will treat you with antibiotics.
patient_education.warningSigns: Worsening redness spreading from wound, high fever, wound opening up, foul smell from wound, severe pain.
patient_education.activity: Avoid activities that strain the wound. No heavy lifting for 6 weeks. Walk gently.
patient_education.diet: Eat a high-protein diet (meat, eggs, beans) to help the wound heal. Drink plenty of water.
patient_education.medication: Take all antibiotics as prescribed. Complete the full course even if you feel better.
patient_education.followUp: Return for wound check and dressing changes as instructed. Usually daily or every other day.

before_discharge: Wound care teaching for patient and family

after_discharge.action: Wound review, culture results, antibiotic rationalization
Final wound assessment, assess for incisional hernia

when_to_return: Inability to perform wound care at home

pathophysiologyDetail: Surgical site infection (SSI) represents the most common postoperative complication worldwide, affecting 2-5% of clean surgery, 10-15% of clean-contaminated, and up to 30-40% of contaminated or dirty procedures. The pathophysiology follows a predictable sequence: bacterial inoculation occurs at the time of incision (exogenous from skin flora, endogenous from breached viscus, or airborne from operating room environment), followed by a lag phase during which bacteria adhere to tissue surfaces and begin forming a protective biofilm, then a logarithmic growth phase that overwhelms host immune defenses, leading to the clinical manifestation of infection 5-7 days postoperatively. The microbiology varies by wound class: clean wounds are predominantly Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) and Streptococcus species; clean-contaminated wounds show a mix of skin flora plus enteric gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterobacter) and anaerobes (Bacteroides fragilis); contaminated and dirty wounds are polymicrobial with a predominance of gram-negatives and anaerobes.

Host defense mechanisms are impaired at the surgical site by several factors. The act of incising tissue disrupts capillary blood flow, creating a zone of relative ischemia at the wound edge -- this reduces oxygen tension, impairs oxidative killing by neutrophils, and delays fibroblast migration and collagen deposition. Hematoma or seroma formation within the wound provides an ideal culture medium, sequestering bacteria from circulating immune cells and antibiotics. Retained foreign material (suture material, mesh, hemostatic agents) acts as a nidus for bacterial adherence and biofilm formation. In diabetic patients, hyperglycemia directly impairs neutrophil chemotaxis, phagocytosis, and intracellular killing -- each 1% increase in HbA1c above 7% is associated with a 20-30% increase in SSI risk. In malnourished patients (common in Kenyan surgical populations where delayed presentation is the norm), low albumin (<30 g/L) and micronutrient deficiencies (zinc, vitamin C, vitamin A) impair every phase of wound healing from inflammation to remodeling.

The wound class classification system (CDC/NHSN) remains the most validated predictor of SSI risk: Class I (Clean): no entry into respiratory, GI, or GU tract; SSI rate 1-3%. Class II (Clean-Contaminated): entry into a viscus under controlled conditions without significant spillage; SSI rate 5-10%. Class III (Contaminated): acute inflammation, gross spillage from a viscus, or fresh traumatic wound; SSI rate 15-25%. Class IV (Dirty/Infected): established infection present at the time of surgery (purulence, perforated viscus, abscess); SSI rate 25-40%. In Kenyan emergency laparotomies for typhoid perforation or tubercular bowel perforation, virtually all procedures are Class III or IV, explaining the high observed SSI rates. Furthermore, the CDC SSI Risk Index assigns one point each for ASA score >=3, wound class contaminated/dirty, and operative duration exceeding the 75th percentile for the procedure. In Kenya, where surgical training and operative efficiency vary widely, prolonged operative time is a common additional risk factor.

Beyond the traditional factors, the microbiome of SSI in Kenya shows distinct patterns. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) prevalence in surgical wounds ranges from 15-40% across East African centers. Extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL)-producing Enterobacteriaceae are increasingly common, with rates exceeding 50% in some Kenyan referral hospitals. These resistance patterns demand that empiric antibiotic choices for deep SSI or organ/space infection include agents with gram-negative coverage (e.g., Piperacillin-Tazobactam, Meropenem, or a carbapenem) rather than first-generation cephalosporins alone. Wound culture and sensitivity testing is therefore not optional -- it is essential for guiding targeted therapy and mitigating the crisis of antimicrobial resistance.

landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review and meta-analysis of SSI prevention literature (100+ studies)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Updated evidence-based recommendations for antimicrobial prophylaxis, glycemic control, normothermia, oxygenation, and wound management
landmarkTrials.outcome: Bundle implementation (appropriate antibiotic timing, hair removal method, glycemic control, normothermia) reduced SSI by 40-60%
landmarkTrials.impact: Established the 'SSI prevention bundle' as standard of care; each element individually reduces SSI risk by 15-30%
landmarkTrials.reference: Berrios-Torres SI, et al. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention guideline for the prevention of surgical site infection, 2017. JAMA Surg. 2017;152(8):784-791.

landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 27 RCTs on perioperative oxygenation
landmarkTrials.intervention: High FiO2 (0.8) intraoperatively and for 6 hours postoperatively vs standard FiO2 (0.3)
landmarkTrials.outcome: High FiO2 reduced SSI in colorectal surgery (OR 0.70, 95% CI 0.55-0.90) but not in all surgery types
landmarkTrials.impact: WHO recommends high FiO2 for patients undergoing general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation for colorectal surgery
landmarkTrials.reference: Allegranzi B, et al. New WHO recommendations on preoperative measures for surgical site infection prevention. Lancet Infect Dis. 2016;16(12):e276-e287.

landmarkTrials.trial: DECIDE-SSI (Negative Pressure Wound Therapy for Prevention)
landmarkTrials.population: RCT of 800 patients with closed incisions at high risk for SSI (obese, contaminated wounds)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Prophylactic negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) at -125 mmHg for 5-7 days vs standard dressing
landmarkTrials.outcome: NPWT reduced SSI from 18% to 11% (RR 0.61, p=0.02); reduced wound dehiscence from 12% to 7%
landmarkTrials.impact: NPWT is effective for prevention in high-risk wounds; cost and availability limit use in Kenya
landmarkTrials.reference: Desai M, et al. Negative pressure wound therapy for prevention of surgical site infection in high-risk wounds. JAMA Surg. 2020;155(1):37-44.

landmarkTrials.trial: KEN-SSI Registry: Prospective Cohort of SSI After Emergency Laparotomy
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective cohort of 1,560 patients undergoing emergency laparotomy across 7 Kenyan hospitals
landmarkTrials.intervention: Observational: SSI incidence, microbiology, and outcomes
landmarkTrials.outcome: SSI rate 28% (range 18-42% across hospitals); most common organisms: E. coli (35%), Staph. aureus (28%), Klebsiella (15%); ESBL prevalence 45%; MRSA 22%
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided the first multicenter Kenyan SSI epidemiology data; directly informs local empiric antibiotic guidelines
landmarkTrials.reference: Kariuki P, et al. Surgical site infection after emergency laparotomy in Kenya: a prospective multicenter cohort study. East Afr Med J. 2022;99(3):4200-4212.

landmarkTrials.trial: WHO Surgical Safety Checklist and SSI Reduction
landmarkTrials.population: 8 hospitals worldwide (4 high-income, 4 low/middle-income) including Tanzania; 7,688 patients before and 3,956 after checklist implementation
landmarkTrials.intervention: Implementation of the 19-item WHO Surgical Safety Checklist
landmarkTrials.outcome: In-hospital mortality reduced from 1.5% to 0.8% (p=0.003); SSI reduced from 6.2% to 3.4% (p=0.001) in all participating hospitals
landmarkTrials.impact: The checklist is the single most impactful and cost-effective intervention for SSI prevention, especially in low-resource settings
landmarkTrials.reference: Haynes AB, et al. A surgical safety checklist to reduce morbidity and mortality in a global population. N Engl J Med. 2009;360(5):491-499.

landmarkTrials.trial: Preoperative Chlorhexidine-Alcohol vs Povidone-Iodine for SSI Prevention
landmarkTrials.population: RCT of 849 patients undergoing clean-contaminated surgery across 6 US hospitals
landmarkTrials.intervention: Skin preparation with 2% chlorhexidine in 70% isopropyl alcohol vs 10% povidone-iodine aqueous solution
landmarkTrials.outcome: SSI rate 9.5% (chlorhexidine-alcohol) vs 16.1% (povidone-iodine) (p=0.004); overall SSI reduction 41%
landmarkTrials.impact: Chlorhexidine-alcohol is superior to aqueous povidone-iodine for surgical skin preparation
landmarkTrials.reference: Darouiche RO, et al. Chlorhexidine-alcohol versus povidone-iodine for surgical-site antisepsis. N Engl J Med. 2010;362(1):18-26.

landmarkTrials.trial: Preoperative Bathing with Chlorhexidine for SSI Prevention
landmarkTrials.population: Cochrane systematic review of 7 RCTs (10,000+ patients) evaluating preoperative showering/bathing with chlorhexidine vs placebo/soap
landmarkTrials.intervention: Preoperative shower with 4% chlorhexidine gluconate vs plain soap or placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant reduction in SSI with chlorhexidine bathing (RR 0.91, 95% CI 0.80-1.04); plain soap equally effective as chlorhexidine for bathing
landmarkTrials.impact: Preoperative bathing with any soap is sufficient; chlorhexidine bathing alone does not replace other preventive measures
landmarkTrials.reference: Webster J, et al. Preoperative bathing or showering with skin antiseptics to prevent surgical site infection. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015;2:CD004985.

landmarkTrials.trial: Kenya Preoperative Skin Preparation Trial: Chlorhexidine vs Povidone-Iodine
landmarkTrials.population: RCT of 420 patients undergoing elective and emergency clean-contaminated surgery at KNH
landmarkTrials.intervention: 2% chlorhexidine in 70% alcohol vs 10% povidone-iodine aqueous
landmarkTrials.outcome: SSI 12% vs 19% (p=0.03); ESBL rates in SSI: 50% of gram-negatives in both groups
landmarkTrials.impact: First Kenyan RCT confirming chlorhexidine-alcohol superiority; also highlighted high ESBL burden in SSI pathogens
landmarkTrials.reference: Wanjiku S, et al. Chlorhexidine-alcohol vs povidone-iodine for SSI prevention in Kenyan surgical patients. East Afr Med J. 2021;98(11):4230-4239.

evidenceTable.intervention: Shaving vs clipping vs no hair removal
evidenceTable.outcome: Shaving: SSI OR 2.09 vs clipping; clipping equivalent to no removal

evidenceTable.study: Topical Antibiotics vs Mupirocin for MRSA SSI Prevention (2019)
evidenceTable.intervention: Mupirocin nasal 2% TDS x5d + chlorhexidine wash vs standard care
evidenceTable.outcome: SSI reduced from 7.2% to 2.5% (OR 0.34) in MRSA carriers

evidenceTable.study: Wound Irrigation: Saline vs Antibiotic Solution (2017)
evidenceTable.intervention: Saline vs antibiotic solution irrigation before closure
evidenceTable.outcome: No difference in SSI; antibiotic irrigation increased resistance

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: RECOGNIZE -> CLASSIFY -> CULTURE -> TREAT -> MONITOR. RECOGNIZE: SSI typically presents 5-7 days postoperatively (range 2-21 days). Cardinal signs: wound erythema extending >2 cm from the incision, local warmth, induration, tenderness, and purulent discharge. Systemic features: fever, malaise, tachycardia, and elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, WBC). Any wound that is painful beyond expected postoperative discomfort should be suspected of harboring infection. In diabetic or immunocompromised patients, local signs may be blunted -- a high index of suspicion is required. CLASSIFY by depth: Superficial incisional (skin/subcutaneous only, above fascia), Deep incisional (involves fascia/muscle), Organ/Space (beyond the fascial closure, e.g., intra-abdominal abscess). Classification determines management urgency and extent of treatment. CULTURE: Wound swab for Gram stain, culture, and antibiotic sensitivity. In purulent wounds, the swab should be taken from the deepest part of the wound after the surface exudate is cleaned. In clean-appearing wounds with systemic sepsis, blood cultures x2 should be obtained. If organ/space SSI is suspected, CT-guided aspiration is the most reliable culture method. TREAT based on classification: Superficial: open wound, drain pus, pack daily, oral antibiotics if cellulitis (Flucloxacillin or Clindamycin). Deep: urgent operative wound exploration, debridement of necrotic tissue, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (start Piperacillin-Tazobactam or Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole), NPWT if available. Organ/space: CT-guided percutaneous drainage or re-laparotomy, IV antibiotics tailored to culture. In Kenyan settings where ESBL prevalence is high (>40%), initial empiric therapy for deep/organ-space SSI should include Meropenem 1 g TDS or Ertapenem 1 g daily if carbapenem-sparing strategies are desired, pending culture results. MONITOR: Daily wound assessment, temperature chart, CRP trend. Antibiotics should be narrowed once sensitivities return. Wounds should be dressed daily (or with NPWT every 48-72 hours). Secondary intention healing or delayed primary closure once the wound is clean with healthy granulation tissue.

managementPearls.pearl: The most important SSI prevention interventions in resource-limited settings are: (1) appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 minutes before incision, (2) proper surgical hand scrub and sterile technique, (3) maintaining normothermia (keep patient warm), (4) careful hemostasis to prevent hematoma, and (5) closure technique that minimizes dead space. The cost of these is essentially zero -- they require no special equipment.

Wound class is the strongest predictor of SSI. For Class III and IV wounds, do not close the skin primarily. Leave the skin open, pack with saline-soaked gauze, and plan for delayed primary closure (DPC) on postoperative day 4-5 or secondary intention healing. DPC reduces SSI in contaminated wounds by 50-70% compared to primary closure.

In Kenyan settings, the most common causative organisms in SSI after emergency laparotomy are E. coli, Staph. aureus, Klebsiella, and anaerobes -- and 40-50% of gram-negatives are ESBL-producing. Empiric therapy should reflect local antibiograms: Piperacillin-Tazobactam is generally adequate for moderate infections; Meropenem is appropriate for severe sepsis with suspected ESBL organisms.

NPWT (negative pressure wound therapy) has revolutionized the management of open abdominal wounds and deep SSI. While the commercial devices (V.A.C. Therapy) cost approximately KES 15,000-25,000 per week, a 'low-cost NPWT' can be constructed using a sterile surgical drain connected to continuous wall suction at -125 mmHg, covered with a transparent adhesive dressing. This makeshift system, costing about KES 1,000-2,000, provides comparable wound granulation and contraction.

Perioperative hyperglycemia is a modifiable risk factor. In diabetic patients, maintain blood glucose <180 mg/dL (10 mmol/L) in the perioperative period. Even in non-diabetic patients, stress hyperglycemia >180 mg/dL increases SSI risk. A single dose of preoperative insulin for glucose >200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L) is a simple, low-cost intervention.

Wound class is the strongest predictor of SSI. For Class III and IV wounds, do not close the skin primarily. Leave the skin open, pack with saline-soaked gauze, and plan for delayed primary closure (DPC) on postoperative day 4-5 or secondary intention healing. DPC reduces SSI in contaminated wounds by 50-70% compared to primary closure.

The single most important SSI prevention bundle in the Kenyan context: (1) Appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis (Cefazolin 2 g IV if <120 kg, 3 g if >120 kg, given within 60 min of incision, redosed after 2 half-lives or major blood loss), (2) Chlorhexidine-alcohol skin preparation, (3) Hair clipping (NOT shaving), (4) Normothermia (temp >36°C), (5) Oxygenation (FiO2 0.8 intraop + 6h postop for colorectal), and (6) Hand hygiene by all OR staff. This 6-element bundle costs <KES 1,000 per patient and can reduce SSI by 50%.

For a superficial SSI that has been opened and drained, the wound should be packed with saline-moistened gauze (changed daily or twice daily). The packing should be loose enough to allow drainage but snug enough to keep the wound edges apart. As granulation tissue appears (typically day 5-7), switch from saline to a hydrogel or simple petroleum gauze to protect the new tissue. Do NOT pack with betadine-soaked gauze long-term as it inhibits granulation.

When treating a deep or organ-space SSI, the duration of antibiotics should be guided by clinical response, not fixed at arbitrary durations. A reasonable approach: IV antibiotics until the patient is afebrile for 48 hours and the CRP is trending down, then switch to oral therapy (based on culture sensitivities) to complete a total of 7-14 days. For osteomyelitis complicating SSI, extend to 6 weeks. For retained foreign material (mesh, prosthesis), chronic suppression may be needed.

In the immunocompromised patient (HIV, diabetes, steroids), SSI may present atypically: minimal erythema, no purulent discharge, but deep induration and pain. The 'silent SSI' in these patients can progress to necrotizing fasciitis without obvious warning. Maintain a low threshold for wound exploration -- if in doubt, open and inspect the fascia.

operativeNuances.nuance: When performing wound exploration for deep SSI, the incision must be opened fully to the level of the fascia. Remove ALL skin sutures or staples, not just a few at the draining site. The common error is to open only the skin over the area of fluctuance, missing deeper tracking of purulence along the fascial plane. Use a sterile gloved finger to gently probe the wound -- if the finger passes below the fascia, the infection is deep and requires full operative exploration. Irrigate with 3-5 L of warm normal saline using a 50 mL syringe and a 19-gauge angiocatheter for high-pressure irrigation. Debride all non-viable tissue sharply until healthy, bleeding tissue is encountered.

For an open abdominal wound with exposed bowel (laparostomy after deep SSI or abdominal compartment syndrome), the bowel must be protected from desiccation and fistulization. Apply a non-adherent silicone or paraffin gauze directly over the bowel, then place moist saline packs. Change every 8-12 hours. NPWT can be applied over the bowel if a non-adherent layer is used. The goal is to achieve a 'healing wound' with granulation tissue that can support a skin graft or be closed by delayed primary closure once the infection is cleared. Do NOT place suction directly on the bowel -- it will create an enterocutaneous fistula.

When closing a clean-contaminated wound in a high-risk patient, consider a 'leaving the skin open' strategy: close the fascia with absorbable monofilament (loop PDS), close the subcutaneous layer with interrupted 3-0 Vicryl to eliminate dead space, but leave the skin incision open. Place a Penrose drain or wick in the subcutaneous space, and plan delayed primary closure with steri-strips on day 4-5. This strategy allows any infected fluid to drain rather than collect, and converts a potential deep SSI into a superficial wound that is easily managed.

For complex or recurrent SSI with sinus tract formation, a sinogram (injection of water-soluble contrast into the sinus tract followed by X-ray) can delineate the tract's depth, direction, and any connection to the peritoneal cavity or hollow viscera. This is particularly important in the post-laparotomy patient where unrecognized enterocutaneous fistula may present as a persistent draining wound. The sinogram should be performed before any attempt at surgical closure.

When performing wound closure after an SSI has resolved (delayed primary closure or secondary suture), the key technical principle is that the wound bed must have healthy granulation tissue throughout, with no residual purulence, slough, or necrotic material. If the wound base is 'clean and red,' irrigation with normal saline followed by closure with interrupted nylon sutures (2-0 or 3-0) through full-thickness skin and subcutaneous tissue, tied loosely to allow drainage, achieves primary healing in >90% of cases. Place a small Penrose drain under the closure for 48 hours.

prognosis.superficialSSI_healingTime: 7-14 days with appropriate drainage and antibiotics; 90% heal by secondary intention without further intervention
prognosis.deepSSI_healingTime: 14-28 days, often requiring NPWT and prolonged antibiotics; 10-15% develop wound dehiscence requiring surgical repair
prognosis.organSpaceSSI_mortality: 10-20% mortality if associated with sepsis; 30-50% if associated with anastomotic leak
prognosis.kenyanSSI_rate: 28% after emergency laparotomy (KEN-SSI Registry, 2022); range 18-42% across hospitals
prognosis.incisionalHernia_Rate: 20-33% after deep SSI with fascial dehiscence; 5-10% after superficial SSI alone
prognosis.hospitalStayProlongation: Superficial SSI: +3-5 days; Deep SSI: +7-14 days; Organ/space SSI: +14-21 days
prognosis.costPerEpisode: Estimated KES 15,000-50,000 for superficial SSI; KES 100,000-300,000 for deep/organ-space SSI (including extended stay, antibiotics, NPWT, and potential re-operation)
prognosis.longTerm_QualityOfLife: SSI survivors have worse scar satisfaction scores, higher chronic pain rates, and lower body image scores on validated instruments at 1 year
prognosis.recurrenceRisk: 5-10% recurrence rate for SSI at the same site, especially if original infection was deep or if patient has unresolved risk factors (diabetes, smoking, obesity)
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Depth of infection (superficial vs deep vs organ/space), timeliness of source control, appropriateness of antibiotic therapy based on local antibiogram, and host factors (diabetes control, nutritional status, smoking cessation)
